{"id":"beefcake-7r69","title":"Epic: Ensemble Swarm — Collaborative Multi-Model Architecture","description":"Transform the swarm from a rigid hierarchy (Opus manager → 122B workers) into a collaborative ensemble where each model contributes its unique strengths per workflow phase.

## Motivation
We have 35+ models across 4 providers but use only 1 cloud + 2 local. GPU utilization is ~43%. Simple issues cost $4.50 each (Opus for everything). The beads ecosystem article states: "No frameworks currently support heterogeneous model assignment."

## Architecture: Phase-Based Model Selection

Each issue flows through phases, each using the best-fit model:
- TRIAGE: Haiku/Flash (fast, cheap) → classify complexity
- EXPLORE: Gemini 3.1 Pro (2M context) → read entire codebase
- PLAN: Opus (strongest reasoning) → produce strategy
- IMPLEMENT: Local workers / MiniMax M2.7 (free or ¢) → write code
- REVIEW: Different model than implementer (diversity)
- VERIFY: Deterministic verifier (no model)

For critical changes: 3-model consensus voting before proceeding.

## Available Fleet
Cloud (via CLIAPIProxy): claude-opus-4-6, claude-sonnet-4-6, claude-haiku-4-5, gemini-3.1-pro-high, gemini-3-flash-preview, gemini-2.5-flash, gpt-5.2-codex, gpt-oss-120b-medium
External (OpenRouter): MiniMax M2.7 ($0.30/M input)
Local: Qwen3.5-122B-A10B × 2 (vasp-01/02), Qwen3-Coder-Next 80B/3B MoE (vasp-03, deploying)

## Estimated Impact
- 80% cost reduction on simple issues (Flash/MiniMax vs Opus)
- 5-6 concurrent issues (up from 3)
- Higher quality on complex issues (multi-model consensus)
- Self-improving routing (learns which model handles what best)

## Key Patterns Adopted
- ruflo: Q-learning router, heterogeneous model routing, token optimization
- Foolery: Weighted agent pools per workflow stage
- claude-flow: Consensus mechanisms for critical decisions
- Robin: Active feedback injection from past outcomes
- Aviary: Dense reward signals for granular feedback","notes":"## Postmortem (2026-03-24T12:18:00Z)

ROOT CAUSE:
The orchestrator's pre-filter classifier matched on the issue type field containing the string "epic" and applied a hard-coded rejection rule that treats epics as non-code tasks, exiting in under one second before any decomposition or implementation work could begin. The swarm never evaluated the issue body, which contains concrete, file-level implementation instructions.

PATTERN:
This is a pre-filter false rejection — a gate-blocking pattern where a classifier keyed on issue metadata (type label or title keyword) discards implementable work by conflating project-management taxonomy with technical feasibility.

FIX STRATEGY:
- Grep the codebase for the exact string "epic (not a code task)" to locate the rejection branch (almost certainly in crates/swarm_agents/src/orchestrator.rs inside a function named pre_filter, should_skip, or reject_issue), then change that branch from a hard Err/None return to a conditional: if the issue body length exceeds 200 characters AND the body contains any of ["implement", "file:", "fn ", "struct ", "enum ", "->"], return a new ProcessingMode::PhaseDecomposition variant instead of rejecting.
- Add a feature flag epic_decomposition: bool to the FeatureFlags struct in crates/swarm_agents/src/config.rs, defaulting to true, read from environment variable SWARM_EPIC_DECOMPOSITION, and gate the new pre-filter behavior on this flag so the decomposition path can be enabled or disabled without recompiling.
- After fixing the gate, implement the PhaseRouter struct in crates/swarm_agents/src/phase_router.rs as described in the issue body, wire it into Orchestrator::new() and the main dispatch function, and add the tests specified in Step 6 so that cargo test -p swarm_agents confirms the fix before the next run.

Title: Fix Pre-Filter False Rejection and Implement Phase-Based Model Router

Context for the automated agent:
Issue beefcake-7r69 was rejected by the orchestrator pre-filter in under one second because the issue type field contained the string "epic." The rejection log line reads: Pre-filter: rejecting issue the swarm cannot handle, reason="epic (not a code task)". That rejection is wrong. This issue contains concrete, file-level implementation work. Do not re-triage this as an epic or non-code task. Process it as a standard feature implementation. The steps below are ordered so that the pre-filter fix comes first, unblocking all subsequent work.

Step 0 — Find the rejection site.
Run: grep -rn "epic (not a code task)" crates/swarm_agents/src/
The result will give you the exact file and line number. It will almost certainly be in crates/swarm_agents/src/orchestrator.rs or crates/swarm_agents/src/orchestrator/mod.rs, inside a function named pre_filter, should_skip, or reject_issue. Open that file and read the entire function before making any edits.

Step 1 — Add the PhaseDecomposition processing mode.
File: wherever the ProcessingMode (or equivalent) enum is defined — grep for "enum ProcessingMode" or look for Normal/Skip variants near the pre-filter function.
Add a new variant: PhaseDecomposition.
If no such enum exists and the function returns a bool or Option, introduce the enum now so the pre-filter can signal decomposition rather than rejection.

Step 2 — Fix the pre-filter.
File: the file found in Step 0, function found in Step 0.
Locate the branch that matches issue type == "epic" (or a string contains check on "epic").
Replace the immediate rejection return with the following logic:

  let body = issue.body.as_deref().unwrap_or("");
  let has_code_signals = ["implement", "file:", "fn ", "struct ", "enum ", "->"]
      .iter()
      .any(|s| body.contains(s));
  if body.len() > 200 && has_code_signals {
      if flags.epic_decomposition {
          return Ok(ProcessingMode::PhaseDecomposition);
      }
  }
  // only reach the original rejection if body is short or has no code signals
  return Err(Rejected { reason: "epic (not a code task)".into() });

This preserves the original rejection for genuine non-code epics while allowing decomposable ones through.

Step 3 — Add the feature flag.
File: crates/swarm_agents/src/config.rs
Find the FeatureFlags struct (the run log shows it exists: "Feature flags loaded flags=smart_router=OFF state_machine=OFF ...").
Add field: pub epic_decomposition: bool
In the constructor or from_env function that reads the other flags, add:
  epic_decomposition: std::env::var("SWARM_EPIC_DECOMPOSITION")
      .map(|v| v == "true" || v == "1")
      .unwrap_or(true),
Default is true so the fix is active on the next run without any environment change.

Step 4 — Create the PhaseRouter.
File: crates/swarm_agents/src/phase_router.rs (create new file)

Define:

  use std::collections::HashMap;

  #[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]
  pub enum WorkflowPhase {
      Triage,
      Explore,
      Plan,
      Implement,
      Review,
      Verify,
  }

  #[derive(Debug, Clone)]
  pub struct ModelSpec {
      pub endpoint: String,
      pub model: String,
      pub cost_per_million_tokens: f64,
  }

  pub struct PhaseRouter {
      map: HashMap<WorkflowPhase, ModelSpec>,
  }

  impl PhaseRouter {
      pub fn default_with_config(config: &crate::config::Config) -> Self {
          let mut map = HashMap::new();
          map.insert(WorkflowPhase::Triage, ModelSpec {
              endpoint: config.fast_endpoint.clone(),
              model: "gemini-2.5-flash".into(),
              cost_per_million_tokens: 0.075,
          });
          map.insert(WorkflowPhase::Explore, ModelSpec {
              endpoint: config.cloud_endpoint.clone(),
              model: "gemini-3.1-pro-high".into(),
              cost_per_million_tokens: 1.25,
          });
          map.insert(WorkflowPhase::Plan, ModelSpec {
              endpoint: config.cloud_endpoint.clone(),
              model: "claude-opus-4-6".into(),
              cost_per_million_tokens: 15.0,
          });
          map.insert(WorkflowPhase::Implement, ModelSpec {
              endpoint: config.coder_endpoint.clone(),
              model: "qwen3.5-122b".into(),
              cost_per_million_tokens: 0.0,
          });
          map.insert(WorkflowPhase::Review, ModelSpec {
              endpoint: config.cloud_endpoint.clone(),
              model: "claude-sonnet-4-6".into(),
              cost_per_million_tokens: 3.0,
          });
          // Verify is deterministic — no model
          Self { map }
      }

      pub fn select(&self, phase: &WorkflowPhase) -> Option<&ModelSpec> {
          self.map.get(phase)
      }
  }

{"notes":"## Postmortem (2026-03-24T13:50:52Z)

ROOT CAUSE: All three subtask workers exhausted their per-subtask time budget (~16 minutes each) doing read-only exploration without writing a single edit. The planner decomposed the work incorrectly — it assigned the PhaseModelSelector struct to triage.rs, but triage.rs already contains a complete PhaseModelSelector implementation (visible in the read_file output at lines 103-141+). The agents spent their entire budget re-reading files trying to understand what already existed rather than making targeted edits.

PATTERN: This is a context-deficit / wrong-decomposition failure. The planner hallucinated a task structure (PhaseModelSelector missing from triage.rs, needs adding to reformulation.rs and patch_tool.rs) that did not match reality. The agents then burned their budgets doing reconnaissance on files that either already had the target code or had no clear connection to the feature.

FIX STRATEGY:
- Read config.rs and agents/mod.rs first — the issue description explicitly names these as the target files, but the planner ignored them and assigned work to triage.rs, reformulation.rs, and patch_tool.rs instead. The actual gap is in config.rs (PhaseModelSelector wiring) and agents/mod.rs (per-phase agent construction), not in the files the planner chose.
- Before decomposing into subtasks, the planner must grep for existing PhaseModelSelector, WorkflowPhase, and SwarmStackProfile usages across the codebase (use list_files + read_file on config.rs and agents/mod.rs) to establish ground truth about what exists vs. what is missing, then only assign subtasks to files that actually need changes.
- Each subtask must be given a strict read budget: no more than 2 read_file calls before the first edit_file call. If a subtask cannot identify its edit target within 2 reads, it should fail fast rather than continue exploring.

REVISED DESCRIPTION:

Phase-based model selector: wire PhaseModelSelector into config.rs and agents/mod.rs

Context: triage.rs already contains the complete PhaseModelSelector struct, WorkflowPhase enum, Complexity enum, and TriageResult struct. Do NOT modify triage.rs. The dependency issue beefcake-5141 has already wired the multi-endpoint model catalog. The remaining work is to connect PhaseModelSelector into the configuration layer and agent construction layer.

Step 1 — Read these two files before doing anything else:
  crates/swarm-agents/src/config.rs
  crates/swarm-agents/src/agents/mod.rs

Step 2 — In config.rs:
  Find the SwarmStackProfile struct or equivalent hardcoded SwarmRole→model mapping (search for "HybridBalancedV1" or "SwarmStackProfile" or "SwarmRole"). Replace or augment it with a PhaseModelSelector field. The config struct that builds agents (likely named SwarmConfig or AgentConfig or similar) should gain a field:
    pub phase_selector: PhaseModelSelector,
  Construct it from the existing CloudModelCatalog and the SWARM_MAX_COST_PER_ISSUE environment variable (parse as f64, default to 0.0 meaning unlimited):
    let max_cost = std::env::var("SWARM_MAX_COST_PER_ISSUE").ok().and_then(|v| v.parse::<f64>().ok()).unwrap_or(0.0);
    phase_selector: PhaseModelSelector::new(catalog.clone(), max_cost),
  Import PhaseModelSelector from crate::triage::PhaseModelSelector.

Step 3 — In agents/mod.rs:
  Find the function that builds agents per SwarmRole (likely named build_agents, build_swarm, or similar — search for "SwarmRole" or "HybridBalancedV1" or "rust_coder"). Replace the fixed model string lookups with calls to phase_selector.select_for_phase(phase, triage.as_ref()). The mapping is:
    WorkflowPhase::Triage    → triage agent (cheap flash model)
    WorkflowPhase::Explore   → explorer agent (fast local or medium cloud)
    WorkflowPhase::Plan      → planner agent (high-capability cloud, e.g. opus/sonnet)
    WorkflowPhase::Implement → coder agent (code-specialized model)
    WorkflowPhase::Review    → reviewer agent (must differ from implementer)
  When select_for_phase returns None, fall back to the existing local endpoint model string that was previously hardcoded.
  The function signature should accept a phase_selector: &PhaseModelSelector and triage: Option<&TriageResult> parameter, or read them from the config struct.

Step 4 — Verify the build compiles:
  Run: cargo check -p swarm-agents
  Fix any import errors (use crate::triage::{PhaseModelSelector, WorkflowPhase, TriageResult}).
  Do not modify triage.rs, reformulation.rs, or patch_tool.rs.

Files to edit: crates/swarm-agents/src/config.rs and crates/swarm-agents/src/agents/mod.rs only.
Files to read first: same two files, plus a targeted grep for "SwarmStackProfile" and "HybridBalancedV1" to locate the exact structs/functions to replace."}
